content.title: Cauchy Sequence
content.content:
A sequence $(x_n)$ in $(X, d)$ is **Cauchy** 
if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that 
$m, n \geq N$ implies $d(x_m, x_n) < \varepsilon$.

content.title: Complete Metric Space
content.content:
A metric space $(X, d)$ is **complete** 
if every Cauchy sequence converges to a point in $X$.

content.title: Complete and Incomplete Spaces
content.problem:
1. $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard metric is complete.

2. $(0, 1)$ with the standard metric is not complete: $(1/n)$ is Cauchy 
but converges to $0 \notin (0, 1)$.

3. $\mathbb{Q}$ with the standard metric is not complete.

4. $C[a, b]$ with the supremum metric is complete.

content.title: $C[a,b]$ Is Complete
content.content: $(C[a, b], d_\infty)$ is a complete metric space.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $(f_n)$ be Cauchy in $C[a, b]$. For each $x$, $(f_n(x))$ is Cauchy in 
$\mathbb{R}$ (since $|f_m(x) - f_n(x)| \leq d_\infty(f_m, f_n)$). Define 
$f(x) = \lim f_n(x)$.

We show $f_n \to f$ uniformly. Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $N$ so that 
$d_\infty(f_m, f_n) < \varepsilon/2$ for $m, n \geq N$. For any $x$:
\[
|f_n(x) - f(x)| = \lim_{m \to \infty} |f_n(x) - f_m(x)| \leq \frac{\varepsilon}{2} < \varepsilon.
\]
Thus $d_\infty(f_n, f) \to 0$. Since uniform limits of continuous functions 
are continuous, $f \in C[a, b]$.

content.title: Totally Bounded
content.content:
$(X, d)$ is **totally bounded** if for every 
$\varepsilon > 0$, there exist finitely many points $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ 
such that $X = \bigcup_{i=1}^n B_\varepsilon(x_i)$.

content.title: Characterization of Compactness
content.content:
In a metric space, the following are equivalent:

1. $X$ is compact (every open cover has a finite subcover).

2. $X$ is sequentially compact (every sequence has a convergent subsequence).

3. $X$ is complete and totally bounded.
content.label: Theorem
